af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Lightstep (ServiceNow) distributed tracing and observability platform. Simple API key. Queries, services, deployments, saved queries management. Integration with OpenTelemetry for trace ingestion. Rate limits documented. Dashboard and alert management via API. Note: Lightstep has been integrated into ServiceNow's observability suite — check product roadmap. Good for observability automation agents requiring trace analysis and alerting.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — limited scope granularity. ServiceNow maintained. SOC2 compliance for observability platform. API key via environment variable. Trace data may contain sensitive application internals — access control critical. No read-only API key option documented. ServiceNow acquisition may affect API roadmap.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. ServiceNow integration may introduce API changes — monitor product announcements. Rate limits documented — design accordingly. Trace ingestion and query operations are idempotent. Dashboard and alert changes have operational impact.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for LINE messaging platform (quality 65). API key (channel access token + channel secret). Send messages, reply to events, manage rich menus. Rate limits from LINE Messaging API (1000 msg/sec broadcast). LINE channel must be configured in developer console. Webhook for receiving messages requires HTTPS endpoint. LINE bots have complex event model (message, follow, unfollow, postback). Popular in Japanese/Asian markets. Good for LINE automation agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Channel access token + channel secret pair. Community maintained — dependency hygiene depends on maintainer. LINE requires webhook HTTPS with valid TLS cert — enforces TLS on inbound events. API key and channel secret via environment variables. Webhook signature verification required for security. User data subject to LINE's privacy policies and Japanese data protection law.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: LINE SaaS with good uptime history. API versioning stable. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Webhook signature verification required — invalid signatures cause failures. Rate limits (1000/sec broadcast) require careful management for bulk sends. Channel access token has expiry — must refresh or use long-lived tokens. Community MCP server version lags LINE API updates.

af_score_components.notes: Official Linear MCP server (quality 82). GraphQL API with excellent documentation. API key or OAuth2. Issue, project, cycle, label, team, and workflow management. GraphQL enables precise data fetching — no over-fetching. Rate limits documented. Webhook support for issue events. Linear's keyboard-shortcut-centric UX translates well to agent automation. Powerful for engineering team automation agents. GraphQL schema introspection enables dynamic query generation.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key or OAuth2 with Linear scopes. OAuth enables read/write scope separation. Linear maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed (SOC2 likely). API key via environment variable. Team data access governed by Linear workspace permissions. Webhook HMAC signature verification available. GraphQL mutations for data modification require careful scope control.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable GraphQL API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. GraphQL queries idempotent. Mutations (issue creation, updates) not always idempotent — use optimistic IDs for deduplication. Rate limits by workspace and operation. Webhook delivery reliable. Official MCP server coordinated with API updates — good reliability posture.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for Linear (quality 60) — separate from official linear-api. API key auth. Covers core Linear operations via GraphQL. Less comprehensive than official MCP server. Rate limits from Linear API apply. Documentation relies on Linear API docs. Error handling less sophisticated than official implementation. Consider using official linear-api MCP server instead. May have feature gaps or lag behind Linear API updates.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS via Linear API. API key auth. Community maintained — less rigorous security review than official implementation. API key via environment variable. Limited scope granularity. Inherits Linear API security for backend communications. Prefer official linear-api MCP server for production use.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Reliability depends on Linear API uptime. Community MCP server may lag official API updates. No formal change policy. Error propagation from Linear GraphQL API through MCP layer. Less tested than official implementation. Consider official linear-api MCP server for production workflows.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server using LinkedIn browser session cookies (quality 50). CRITICAL: Browser session auth violates LinkedIn's Terms of Service. LinkedIn actively blocks scraping and may ban accounts. Rate limits from LinkedIn anti-bot detection are unpredictable. Documentation minimal. Error handling for session expiry is fragile. Very high risk of account termination. Not suitable for any production workflow. The official LinkedIn API requires Partner Program approval and has limited capabilities.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for LinkedIn connections. Browser session cookie as auth — highly insecure for automated use. Cookie exposure risk if stored in env vars. Community maintained with unknown security review. ToS violation risk means account termination possible. Session cookies provide full account access — no scope control. Not recommended for any production use.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Entirely dependent on LinkedIn's anti-bot measures not blocking the session. Can fail without notice when LinkedIn updates detection. Session cookies expire — frequent re-authentication needed. Not suitable for production workflows. ToS violation risk makes reliability assessment moot. Extremely fragile approach to LinkedIn automation.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Akamai Cloud (Linode) infrastructure API — Linodes (VMs), NodeBalancers, Kubernetes (LKE), object storage. Simple API key with read-only or full access. Rate limits documented. Async operations require polling action status. Good developer-friendly documentation. OpenAPI spec available. SSH key management. Firewall and networking management. Good cost-effective alternative to major cloud providers for agent-managed infrastructure.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key with read-only or read-write access. Limited scope granularity beyond read/write split. Akamai maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance. API key via environment variable. Read-only tokens for monitoring agents. Infrastructure management has high blast radius — agents can delete Linodes. No per-resource scoping available.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Cloud SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API with OpenAPI spec. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Async Linode operations require polling action status. Rate limits documented — design bulk operations accordingly. Linode creation not idempotent — check existence before creating. Object storage operations idempotent (S3-compatible). Good overall reliability posture.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server (quality 50) — appears to be a temporary file hosting/pastebin-type tool. No auth required for basic operations. Limited documentation. No rate limits published. Very low existing security score (30) indicates minimal security controls. Not suitable for sensitive data. Error handling minimal. File expiry management required. Niche use case for temporary file sharing in agent workflows.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS depends on deployment configuration. No authentication for file operations. No scope control. Community maintained with unknown security review. No compliance certifications. Files uploaded may be publicly accessible — never upload sensitive data. Ephemeral storage means data loss is expected. Not suitable for confidential or regulated data.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA. Unknown version. No documented change policy. File operations have TTL — uploaded files expire. Upload idempotency not guaranteed. Very limited error recovery. Not suitable for production agent workflows requiring reliable storage. Useful only for ephemeral, non-sensitive data in development workflows.

af_score_components.notes: Not an API — this is a reference handbook or documentation resource about LLM agents ecosystem. No MCP server. No authentication required. Not designed for agent integration as a service. Very low scores reflect that this is reference documentation rather than an executable API or tool. Cannot be integrated into agent workflows as a callable service. Use for human reference only.

security_score_components.security_notes: Not an API service — security assessment is not applicable. Static documentation resource. No credentials, no API surface, no network operations. Security score reflects that this is not a deployable service.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Not an API service — reliability assessment not applicable. Static reference documentation. Content may become outdated as the LLM agents ecosystem evolves rapidly. No versioning or change tracking documented.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Logz.io log management and observability platform (built on Elastic). Simple API key. Log ingestion via various endpoints, search API, alert management. Rate limits documented. Kibana-based search queries for log analysis. Account plan determines retention and volume. Good for log-based monitoring agents. Separate API tokens for different features (log ingestion vs search vs account). Monitor token type used for each operation.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key auth — different keys for different features. Limited scope granularity. Logz.io maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance for log management. API key via environment variable. Log data may contain sensitive application internals (PII, credentials in logs) — access control critical. Different token types for ingestion vs search provide minimal feature isolation.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Log ingestion has eventual consistency — recently ingested logs may not be immediately searchable. Volume limits by plan create hard availability boundaries. Rate limits on search API — complex queries consume more quota. Token type confusion (ingestion vs search token) is common source of auth errors.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Loom video messaging and recording platform. OAuth2 only — no API key option for agents. Limited API surface compared to the full Loom feature set. Video upload, trim, space management, sharing. Rate limits documented. Video upload requires resumable upload URL. Integration complexity for video agents. Note: Atlassian acquired Loom — API roadmap may change. Better alternatives exist for video automation if OAuth2 complexity is a barrier.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 only — required for all API access. Limited scope granularity. Atlassian (Loom) maintained. GDPR compliance. OAuth tokens via secure flow. Video content may include sensitive screen recordings — data governance important. No service account pattern documented. Atlassian acquisition may change OAuth implementation. OAuth token refresh required for long-running agents.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API for current features. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. OAuth token refresh required for long-running agents. Video upload requires resumable upload — handle interruptions. Atlassian integration may introduce API changes. Limited feature coverage via API compared to full Loom app. Rate limits on video operations constrain bulk workflows.
